speaker: interviewer
text: With Michael Jackson.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end of this, of this interview, interview, I want to, I want to mention that we, I come from a very large family. My father, my father had-- from on my father's side there were 10, 10 in the family. And my mother's side was 10 in the family. I, for the, for the record, I would like to mention their names, because there were 20 branches of our family. Some of them were totally wiped out. Some of them, one was left. And, and I'll enumerate here each, each, each branch. My father's, my father's, my grandfather's name was Chaim Jakubovics. And my grandmother's name was Miriam Debra. Her maiden name was [? Kirchner. ?] They had ten children-- eight daughters, two sons. From the eldest to the youngest, they were, the eldest daughter was Sara. She was wed to Moshe Leib Heimovics. Those, that branch of the family consisted of 16 souls. Six survived, 10 perished. My father's name, Abraham Zalaman, wed Rivka Drimmer. 14 souls in our immediate family-- 13 perished, one survived. Baila wed Isak Solden. Twelve souls in their family-- 10 perished, two survives. Rifka wed Leib Fixler. 17 souls in their immediate family-- five survived, 12 perished. Chana wed Altar Goldberg. Eight souls in their immediate family-- five perished, three survived. Feige wed Isaak Popper. 14 souls in their immediate family-- 11 perished, three survived. Zacharia wed Sara, my-- her maiden name, I don't remember. They were six souls in their immediate family. They all perished. None survived. Rachel wed Altar Fried. There were nine souls in their family. Eight perished, one survived. Etya wed Isaak Zoltan. Seven souls in their immediate family. Three perished, four survived. Yuta wed Zisse Nejavics. Nine in their family-- all perished, none survived. Total numbers for the, my father's side was 112 members of my father's extended family. 87 perished, 25 survived. Now I'll enumerate from my mother's side. My mother's grandfather's name was Efraim Drimmer. He married Judith. Her head maiden name was Jakubowics. But that was no relations, because in our community, there were at least five or six families that had the name Jakubowics that were not related to each other. The oldest one was called Malka. She wed Berel Jakubowics, no relation. Perished five, six survive-- there were 11 in that family. Rivka, my mother, wed Abraham Zalaman. Already, I listed that with my, in, with my father's side. Yenta, she married Samuel Isaak Davidovics. Seven souls-- six perished, one survived. [? Hirsch ?] Armin Darvas wed [INAUDIBLE], maiden name I don't remember. Eight souls in their immediate family-- six perished, two survived. Kaila wed Lazar Jakubowics, no relation. 10 souls in their immediate family-- nine perished, one survived. Berel wed Mindel [? Markovics. ?] 10 souls in their immediate family-- nine perished, one survived. Lea wed Moshe Josef Weiser. 13 souls in their immediate family-- 12 perished, one survived. Sara was unmarried. One, one soul perished. Altogether from my mother's side, were 59 members, my mother's extended family. 47 perished, 12 survived. All I have to add to this, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Earth, Earth, do not cover their blood. Now, now you want me to talk about, about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why you changed your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS] I, I have to read this here. And now, I want to explain how I went from being Michael and Nina Jakubowics to Michael and Nina Jackson. When we came to Allentown, we were led into the Jewish-- we had no family. We were led into the Jewish Community Center. And the director of the Jewish Community Center in Allentown at that time was Mr., Mr. Feldman. He was the one who said to us, you have to change your last name because Jakubowics is hard to spell, let alone pronounce. Here we were, two bewildered immigrants. And we thought that this man probably knows that changing our name will be helpful and beneficial to our livelihood. That will make it easier for us to make a living. He proposed that we should change it to Jackson, because it's shorter. That was his argument. We accepted his advice. And he asked Mrs. [? Cohen ?] to take us to the local refugee administration office to a Mr. Boyle. There, Mrs. [? Cohen ?] spoke on our behalf and told Mr. Boyle that we want to change our names. This was all in the first few hours that we arrived here in Allentown. About a year or two later, I started to feel regret about our actions. But it was already too late. I thought about it a lot back then and it was, and it was bothered that I, who am the only survivor of my branch of the martyred Jakubowics family, would agree to let my name go. My only explanation is that it didn't change my Jewish name, the name that my parents gave me when I was brought into the covenant of Abraham. When one is called out to the Torah, even in Europe, they did not call somebody up by the name-- by the last name, but rather by the name that is given to him by his parents. I am Michael ben-Abraham Zalaman. It does, it is done in the same way here in the United States. And as far as I know, families, family names that have been Slavic, that have a Slavic sound, were forced onto our great--great--grandparents when they lived in the Slavic-speaking European countries. To sum it all up, although I struggled with it, why I made this decision originally, I can say, I can say now that I'm very happy that I threw away the Slavic name that was forced on my family. And I, I adopted, on my own free will, an American name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I came to America? Why I came to America is, I think that, that I came to America because I didn't want to, to, to force on Nina something that, that she didn't have her, her upbringing. She was raised in a different, in a different environment than I was. I was raised in a Zionist environment. And it would have been, in 1948, it would have been very hard, very hard for her to accept a, a-- she would have gone if I would've insisted on it. But I realized that I have no right to ask her. After all, what we went through, five horrible years, she went through the same thing as I did. In, in, in 1949, Israel was at that time in a struggle of thousand troubled, you know-- in, in, in infancy. And I realized it would be a very, very hard for her especially that doesn't, didn't have the feelings for it, to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, and, and don't we have a, a, a, a right after suffering so many years to have a, a, a, a little easier time for a while? That was, that was the reason why I came to the United States. And besides, besides, I knew that the United States is a democratic country and that's where I, what I wanted to live under. What, what did you do to make a living in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we, we both went to work. Nina and I, we had no children when we, when we came over here. Nina is very talented. She, she, during the war time, she learned how to sew. And, and, and sewing was one of the easiest job. She was able to get a job immediately when she came here to Allentown. And I wasn't. It took, it took about five weeks till I found something, something to work, because I couldn't sew. This is a trade. If you know how to sew, to operate the machine. But later on, I started from, from, from scratch. I, I learned how to handle materials and, and later went to school to learn how to make patterns and, and ladies' blouses and dresses. And we were, we were successful. We worked. Nina worked. And I worked 10 years for, for one company. I worked five years for another one. I worked five years. Nina worked for one company. We worked for the same company together five years until our first, until she got pregnant with, with Jack, our first son.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have two, two wonderful children. The oldest one is Jack. He has an MBA degree. And he is a, a vice president of a communication company in, in the United States. And our daughter is, is, is finishing her, her dissertation for her PhD. Hopefully this year, she'll get her PhD degree. And she hopes to, to continue as a college professor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three, three wonderful grandchildren. And I think that Nina's going to show you the pictures. I-- do you want to-- do you want to show? I'll show you the pictures that we have from, from my parents?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll show all the pictures together after. I want to ask you for any closing remarks you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my closing remarks would be this. We are, we are still, when you, when you stand at the foot of the mountain, when you are stand close to the foot of the mountain, you really cannot see the mountain. You have to, you have to have a distance in order to see. Three, three [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH] recorders of this tragic history stand out. One is Elie Wiesel. The second one is [INAUDIBLE] that writes under the pseudonym [INAUDIBLE]. [? Dinar ?] is his name. And the third one that committed suicide after the war is Primo Levi that captured part of this tremendous, tremendous tragedy. I, my, my remarks is I hope that in the, in the coming generation, there will, there will emerge a, a new, a new Jeremiah that will be able to, to sum up and make sense out of this horrible tragedy that, that happens. I have to, I have to say to, to, to the end. I have to say that I do not, I do not believe that one generation is responsible for the other generation. I cannot, I cannot, and I should not accuse children for what their fathers did. That wouldn't be fair. I know it is, I know it is very hard to say, but we have, we have to, we have to realize that every human being has to be responsible for his own deeds. And, and, and the children cannot be responsible for the parents. And the parents cannot be responsible for the children. They have to do what they can to guide the children to go in the right way. But you cannot punish parents for children or children for parents. That's all, that's all I have to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're quite welcome. There's nothing here. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK, to the, to the right in the rear, we'll start with the rear. This is my Uncle Zacharia, my father's youngest brother. Next to him is his wife's, a brother-in-law. Next is my father. Next to my father in the cap am I. And we have to go, we have to go-- those, there are one, two, three. The next three are three brothers, the sons of my father's sister. That is the second one from the left. And next, the one with the, with the bow tie and the hat, is my brother, oldest brother, Eliezer. In the front seat is my father's sister, Baila.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right. Next, in the middle, is my father's sister Sara's daughter, [? Shoshana. ?] Next to her is my mother with the closed eyes. And next to my mother is my father's sister, Sara. And next to her, to the left, the very left, is my father's sister, Feige. And that's the baby, this is, this is a grandson of, of the, the sisters from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1933. I was 13 years of age in, in this picture. Can you zoom into this? Can you see with the cap? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That was my-- I was 13 years, just, just bar mitzvah that time. OK, that's fine. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK, to the right again, this is my uncle, Zacharia.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the back row. Next, next to him is his daughter, Chana. And she is holding her hand on, on a cousin that is the daughter of Baila from, from [INAUDIBLE]. Next to, next to her is my cousin, Abraham Popper. This is the guy that I said that was captured by the Russians and never-- in the labor camp and never came, and never came back. And the one with the glasses, this is my Aunt Sara's daughter. His name is [? Shoshana. ?] We had her on the other picture. Next to her is my sister-in-law, Sari. And next to her on the left in the back is my oldest brother, Eliezer. Now, we, we go back to that right. That's a little bit too much to the right. OK. In the front is, her name was Sara, too. That's from my uncle Zacharia's, Zacharia's wife. Her last name I don't know. And next to her is, is my Aunt Baila. And that's her grandson she is holding. And next to her is my grandmother, my father's mother. And next to her is my father's sister, Feige. Also, those children are, are from [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: About when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture also taken in the same year. I know why those sisters came together, because this, this picture was taken in the month of Elul, where it's customary that people go to, to cemeteries, to, you know, to the graves of their parents. So the families usually-- that's why these, these sisters came together. Also, from different places they came for that date. So I know it's 1930-- either '33 or '34 the latest. OK, this, this might be the year in 1935. I'm only going to mention the first two from the right, right to left. From the right, this is my cousin, Chana. And next to her is her brother, Josef. And those are the ones, those are the ones that were the children of my Uncle Zacharia. She, Chana, was married at the time of 1941. And we took a survey. She had a, a two or three or two and a half year old, little girl, [INAUDIBLE]. She perished in, in, in August the 27th or 28th in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you-- Where do you think this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: What, this picture? This was taken in Torun, in, in, in Torun. This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show the background again?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is on a stroll. You know, it's not in, in, in, in the-- in the village. You know, it's between, between villages. Many Saturdays, we used to stroll between Torun and [INAUDIBLE]. That's where we used to meet, meet some youth from the other community. This is, I think, a wedding picture, if I'm not mistaken. This is my brother, Moshe. And this is my sister-in-law. Her name was Manci. And I think this was taken in 1926. I found this picture in, in New York. My sister-in-law, she had an older sister that left for the United States in, in, after World War I. And, and I took a guess. And I figured that maybe they send her some pictures. And when I came here, sure enough, this was the picture that they sent her. And they were gracious enough to give it to me. And they said, you don't have to give it back. So that's it.
